@graph.name: Mercedes‑Benz V200d / V220d Engine Era Map

mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes‑Benz V200d / V220d engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned V200d / V220d engine typically costs £3,500–£5,500 supply only. Fitting adds £800–£1,300 depending on the specialist. Customers have reported quotes from £5,000 upwards for engine replacement. Used engines offer the most cost‑effective solution (£800–£1,800), while reconditioned units provide better reliability warranties. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your V‑Class.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes‑Benz V200d / V220d engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends on which engine era your V‑Class has. The pre‑facelift OM651 2.1‑litre diesel is a proven engine but has a well‑documented single timing chain that can stretch — a critical weakness. The facelift OM622 1.6‑litre diesel is a newer design but shares common diesel vulnerabilities: injector seal leaks ('black death'), DPF and EGR clogging, and turbocharger issues. V‑Class vehicles are typically high‑mileage, so a first‑class service history is essential.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes‑Benz V200d / V220d?

acceptedAnswer.text: The most common issues are timing chain stretch on the OM651 (rattling on cold start — can destroy the engine if ignored), injector seal leaks ('black death' — soot and carbon buildup around injectors), DPF clogging (short journeys accelerate wear), EGR valve clogging (soot buildup), turbocharger failure, parking brake cable failure, and power steering issues.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a V200d / V220d engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on the vehicle value and condition. Against a well‑kept facelift V‑Class worth £20,000–£30,000, a £4,000–£5,000 recon is 15–25% of the car's value — worth it. Against a tired pre‑facelift with significant body issues, the same recon may approach our 70% Mercedes‑Benz threshold — get a scrap valuation first. The facelift OM622 cars are generally newer with lower mileage, making them more desirable.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes‑Benz V200d / V220d engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Pre‑facelift (2014–2019): OM651.950 (2.1L). Facelift (2019–present): OM622 (1.6L). Check the VIN plate or vehicle documentation. The engine code is also stamped on the engine block. The V200d produces 136 bhp; the V220d produces 163 bhp.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the OM651 timing chain, or does the engine need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: The timing chain and tensioner must be replaced if worn. A preventative replacement costs £1,500–£3,500. A snapped chain almost always destroys the engine, requiring a £3,500–£6,500+ replacement. At the first sign of cold‑start rattle, book an inspection immediately. Regular oil changes are the key to preventing timing chain issues.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: V‑Class V200d / V220d — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Essential maintenance — catch it before catastrophic failure
Piston‑valve contact means little is left to repair
Essential maintenance — catch before injectors seize
Viable repair — catch it before it damages the engine
Common V‑Class issue — catch before brake system errors appear

buyingChecks.title: Buying This V200d / V220d? Check These First
buyIf: * Post‑facelift (2019+) with the OM622 engine (newer design; lower mileage likely)
* Pre‑facelift with documented timing chain replacement (OM651)
* Full service history with regular oil changes — V‑Class vehicles cover high mileage
* No cold‑start rattling (timing chain health — OM651)
* No black carbon deposits around injectors ("black death" warning)
* Evidence of regular longer runs (DPF health)
* Recent brake system inspection (parking brake cables are a known weakness)
avoidIf: * OM651 with cold‑start rattle and no chain history — assume it's overdue
* Black carbon deposits around injectors — "black death" is developing
* No service history — high‑mileage V‑Class vehicles are unforgiving of neglect
* Short‑journey ownership history — DPF and EGR issues likely
* Engine warning light with no diagnosis — may indicate timing, injector or sensor problems
* Power steering issues — a known V‑Class weakness

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The V200d and V220d are Mercedes‑Benz's full‑size MPV diesel offerings in the W447 V‑Class — a spacious, premium people‑carrier that shares its mechanical underpinnings with the Vito van. The pre‑facelift (2014–2019) uses the OM651 2.1‑litre four‑cylinder diesel — a proven engine with a well‑documented single timing chain that can stretch. The facelift (2019–present) introduced the OM622 1.6‑litre diesel with the same power outputs. Both engines share common diesel vulnerabilities: injector seal leaks ("black death"), DPF and EGR clogging, and turbocharger issues. V‑Class vehicles are typically high‑mileage (often used as taxis or shuttles), so a first‑class service history is essential. With typical V‑Class values ranging from £15,000 (early high‑mileage) to £35,000+ (low‑mileage facelift), an engine replacement is a significant investment — a well‑maintained facelift OM622 or a pre‑facelift OM651 with documented timing chain history is the safest choice.

cta.label: → Compare V200d / V220d engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Mercedes‑Benz V‑Class V200d / V220d (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for rattling or clattering (timing chain tensioner warning — OM651 particularly) — see the map above
* Injector Seals — Inspect for black carbon deposits around injectors ("black death" warning)
* Service History — Including which engine era (OM651 or OM622) and oil‑change discipline — essential for high‑mileage vehicles
* Timing Chain — For OM651: check for any chain work or cold‑start rattle
* DPF Regeneration — Check history; short journeys accelerate wear
* EGR System — Check for any loss of power or warning lights; EGR clogging is common
* Turbo Health — Test acceleration; any lag, whistling, or hesitation?
* Parking Brake — Check operation; parking brake cables are a known V‑Class weakness
* Power Steering — Check for any unusual noises or stiffness
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes read; unresolved issues may indicate timing, injector or sensor problems
* MOT History — Check for emissions‑related failures
